content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 25, 1988

Anthony L. Miller
Chief Deputy
Office of the Secretary of State
1230 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
    Our File No. A-88-257

Dear Mr. Miller:

     You have written on behalf of Secretary of State, March
Fong Eu. Because of certain mandated deadlines, you have
requested that we expedite our response. Consequently, you
have modified your request to one for formal written staff
advice rather than a full Commission opinion.

QUESTIONS

     1. May the Secretary of State sign the certificate of
correctness which is required to appear in the ballot pamphlet?

     2. May the Secretary of State's name appear in the return
address on the ballot pamphlet?

     3. May the Secretary of State's name appear on the cover
of the ballot pamphlet in a statement that the ballot pamphlet
has been prepared by the Secretary of State?

CONCLUSIONS

     1. The Secretary of State may sign the certificate of
correctness which is required to appear in the ballot pamphlet
by Government Code Section 88005(d) and Elections Code section
3574(d). A certification presumably must be signed in order to
carry any legal significance. Therefore, the requirement that
the certificate appear on the ballot pamphlet presumes that it
will be signed by the certifying officer.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Anthony L. Miller
July 25, 1988
Page -2-


     2.  The Secretary of State's name may not appear in the
return address on the ballot pamphlet. Unlike the signature in
the certificate, the return address is sufficient without the
name of the officeholder. The name and address of the office
is enough.

     3.  The Secretary of State's name may not appear on the
cover of the ballot pamphlet where it states "Compiled by MARCH
FONG EU Secretary of State." The voters will be adequately
apprised regarding the pamphlet's preparation if it merely
states "Compiled by the Secretary of State."

                              FACTS

     The Secretary of State is responsible for preparing and
mailing out the California Ballot Pamphlet for each statewide
election. Under the prior version of Government Code Section
89001, since amended by Proposition 73, you sought advice on
her behalf regarding the use of her name in the ballot
pamphlet. (In re Miller (1978) 4 FPPC Ops. 26.)

     In the Miller Opinion, the Commission advised that the
Secretary of State may not sign a letter to the voters which
was included in the ballot pamphlet. The Commission deadlocked
and provided no advice on the related question of whether her
name could appear on the cover of the ballot pamphlet. The
Commission did advise that the Secretary of State could
inscribe her signature on the required certificate of
correctness.

     In the absence of advice from the Commission, the Secretary
of State has continued to place her name on the cover of the
ballot pamphlet, even during periods when she was a candidate
under the former statute. In addition, her name has appeared
in the return address printed on the outside of the ballot
pamphlet.

     You have written to request our advice on these practices
in the wake of the passage of Proposition 73, which amended
Government Code Section 89001. The ballot pamphlet must be
prepared and available for public viewing on or before July 26,
1988. Therefore, you have asked that we expedite the advice to
allow any changes to be made prior to the time that the ballot
pamphlet is made available for public display.

                              ANALYSIS

     Government Code Section 89001 was amended by Proposition
73, effective June 8, 1988. As amended, the section reads:

          No newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
     at public expense.

Anthony L. Miller
July 25, 1988
Page -3-


     As worded, the section could be read to preclude all mass
mailings sent at government expense. We have previously
provided interim advice which determined that the voters did
not intend to eliminate all governmental mass mailings. (See
enclosed copies of Raye Advice Letters, No. A-88-220, June 16,
1988 and July 1, 1988.) We have advised that mass mailings may
be sent if the name of an elected officer does not appear
therein. (Certain narrow exceptions permit some mailings which
do include references to elected officers.)

     Next we apply our interim advice to your specific
questions. The Secretary of State's name is not necessary to
the information on the front cover. It is sufficient to note
that the Secretary of State's office has prepared the ballot
pamphlet. We also conclude that her name is not necessary to
the return address information on the back cover. Again, the
name of the Secretary of State's office is sufficient.
Therefore, our advice is that, for this ballot pamphlet, those
two references to her name should be deleted. Future ballot
pamphlets will be subject to the revised regulation to be
adopted by the Commission at a future date.

     I trust that this letter adequately responds to your
request for expedited advice. If you have any questions
regarding this letter, I may be reached at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel


                                        By: Robert E. Leidigh
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure

Office of the Secretary of State
March Fong Eu

Executive Office
1230 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

(916) 445-6371

JUNE 29, 1988

John H. Larson, Chair
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Request for Opinion (Govt. Code sec 83114):
Govt. Code sec 89001 as amended by Proposition 73
(California Ballot Pamphlet)

Dear Mr. Larson:

On behalf of Secretary of State March Fong Eu, I hereby
request, pursuant to Government Code section 83114, the
opinion of the Fair Political Practices Commission
regarding the publication and mailing of the California
Ballot Pamphlet by the Secretary of State in the context
of the provisions of Proposition 73. Specifically, I
request the Commission's guidance with respect to
questions numbered 2(a), 2(b) and 2(c) initially asked and
partially addressed in opinion No. 77-032 (4 FPPC 26) dated
February 8, 1978. These questions involved the
possibility of using the Secretary of State's name in the
certificate of correctness, the introductory letter and the compilation
reference in the California Ballot Pamphlet after the
Secretary of State had filed a declaration of candidacy
during election years.

In reliance on that opinion, the Secretary of State has,
following the filing of her declaration of candidacy in
those years in which she is a candidate, limited the
inclusion of her name in the California Ballot Pamphlet to
the certificate of correctness, the return address, and
the compilation reference. She has specifically omitted
any reference to her name in the introductory letter. A
copy of the pamphlet published and provided in this manner
in 1986 is enclosed as an example. In the absence of a
Commission opinion or advice to the contrary, she proposes
to continue with this approach with respect to all
subsequent pamphlets.

Please note that, pursuant to Government Code section
88006 and Elections Code section 3576, the final text of
the California Ballot Pamphlet for the November 1988
General Election must be completed and must be available
for public display on or before July 26, 1988. The
issuance of an opinion prior to July 26, 1988 would be
appreciated.

Sincerely,

ANTHONY L. MILLER
Chief Deputy

Enclosure

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 11, 1988


Anthony Miller
Office of the Secretary of State
Executive Office
1230 J Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Re:  88-257

Dear Mr. Miller:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on June 30, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Robert Leidigh, an attorney in
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice. If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed. If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[Signature]

Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel
